topic: A musician hears their song being performed by someone else
raw_output:
[story 1]

The feedback from the monitor was a jagged, ugly thing in my ears. I slammed my fist onto the mixing board, the cheap plastic protesting with a sharp crack. That was it. That was the end of it. I watched the screen, expecting to see the usual stream of clumsy amateurs, but then, a familiar melody, my melody, was pouring out from the speakers. It was being played, perfectly, by some stranger. A cold knot tightened in my stomach, squeezing the air from my lungs. I didn't move, just stared, my jaw clenched so tight my teeth ached. Every note they struck felt like a deliberate erasure of everything I’d poured into those chords. I wanted to rip the speakers apart, to make the sound stop, but my hands were frozen, betraying a frantic need for violence.

[story 2]

I leaned back in my worn leather chair, the stale air of the rehearsal space suddenly thick and suffocating. My knuckles were white where I gripped the edge of the desk. When the music started—my song—it wasn't the raw, passionate rendition I’d envisioned. It was polished, sterile, performed with an easy confidence that felt like a deliberate mockery. A wave of intense resentment washed over me, making my skin feel prickly. I watched the performer, their movements fluid and effortless, and a bitter taste rose in my throat. I stood up abruptly, pacing the small stage, my boots scuffing the floorboards. I didn't speak, just let the silence between the notes speak volumes about my displeasure.

[story 3]

The sound bled through the closed door, unwelcome and invasive. It was my composition, stripped bare, yet twisted into something unrecognizable by these hands. I felt a burning sensation behind my eyes, a pressure building behind my sternum. I didn't bother turning around immediately. Instead, I walked to the window, staring out at the indifferent city lights, my shoulders rigid. When I finally turned back, the sight of the musician playing—someone else inhabiting my creation—ignited a sharp, defensive anger. I crossed my arms tightly over my chest, a posture of absolute rejection. I let out a low, ragged breath, a sound more like a snarl than a sigh, as if the very air was tainted by the performance.
